What It Contains: Original operational data from POS exports.
Why It Matters: Initial source before cleaning, standardization, and consolidation.

What It Contains: Transaction-level payment, sales, tips, refunds, fees, staff, device, and location data.
Why It Matters: Useful for financial validation and transaction-level audit, but too granular for direct daily forecasting.

What It Contains: Item-level product sales, categories, quantities, discounts, taxes, employees, and payment references.
Why It Matters: Useful for product/menu analysis, but not used directly as the final daily demand forecasting table.
Useful to understand order behavior and product composition.

What It Contains: Section/table-level sales, guests, tips, refunds, discounts, and net sales.
Why It Matters: Useful for operational context and service-area analysis.

What It Contains: Daily aggregation of sales, transactions, tips, refunds, and location.
Why It Matters: Converts detailed POS records into daily demand behavior.

What It Contains: Daily POS data enriched with calendar, holiday, weather, event, and seasonality variables.
Why It Matters: Main structured dataset for SARIMAX and external-variable forecasting experiments.

What It Contains: Expanded dataset with candidate engineered variables, lag features, rolling averages, KPI ratios, and staffing pressure indicators.
Why It Matters: This is a candidate feature layer. It does not mean all 115 columns were used as final model features.

What It Contains: Clean dashboard-ready annual sales, transactions, average ticket, weather exposure, holidays, events, and season.
Why It Matters: Used for annual behavior and seasonal business visualization.

What It Contains: Actual vs predicted net sales, transactions, average ticket, KPI-based sales, errors, and accuracy.
Why It Matters: Used for final holdout forecast and manager-facing KPI forecast visualization.

What It Contains: Rolling backtesting actual vs forecast values by validation month.
Why It Matters: Used to show that model evaluation was not based only on a single 30-day window.
